Saurabh runs TheLatexLab, a professional Word to LaTeX and PDF to LaTeX conversion service for researchers and PhD students. He and his team have converted 500+ research papers, theses, and conference submissions to clean, submission-ready LaTeX for IEEE, Elsevier, Springer, ACM, and 200+ other journal templates. ## Keep Reading

Word To Latex Conversion

### How to Convert Word to LaTeX Without Losing Tables and Formatting

April 2026 · 14 min read

Word To Latex Conversion [...] Best Word to Latex Converter For Academic Paper 

There are five real options for converting a Word document to LaTeX in 2026: Pandoc, GrindEQ, Docx2LaTeX, Writer2LaTeX, and professional conversion service. There are also dozens of online converters that are essentially Pandoc wrappers with a drag-and-drop interface. None of them produce submission-ready LaTeX from a complex academic paper without manual cleanup. But they differ significantly in which parts they handle well – equations, tables, citations, or template compliance – and knowing these differences saves you hours of wasted effort with the wrong tool. [...] In this comparison

1. What actually matters in a Word-to-LaTeX converter

6. Mathpix (PDF-to-LaTeX, not Word-to-LaTeX)

10. Frequently asked questions

## What Actually Matters in a Word-to-LaTeX Converter

Most comparison articles list features like “supports bold and italic” or “converts headings.” That’s not useful – every tool handles basic text. What separates them is performance on the hard parts of academic paper conversion:

results.title: How to convert PDF to (La)TeX? - LaTeX Stack Exchange
results.content:
Kasra's user avatar
`mathpix`

Import the file using LibreOffice. It will create a Draw file. You have to copy and edit it by pieces into Writer. Then you can use the plugin `writer2latex` to export the results to LaTeX. The final code is dirty, with plenty of stuff you don't need and some errors.

`writer2latex`

If your docs are large enough, this process might be easier than re-typing everything.

Another alternative, if you are dealing mostly with pure and simple text, is to save the file as a `.txt` from the PDF reader. Then copy/paste the thing into your LaTeX editor.

`.txt`

example.pdf [...] Mohsen Banan's user avatar

It seems that the best solution to convert a pdf to Latex is to use an AI: Mistral.ai ()

rpapa's user avatar

Convert PDF to Latex by acquiring the source `.doc` or `.docx`.

`.doc`
`.docx`

If you can acquire the `.docx` file that generated the `.pdf`, then you can one-shot `.docx` -> `latex` and get decent results using:

`.docx`
`.pdf`
`.docx`
`latex`
`file menu`
`convert`

Alternatively, If you're sure the PDF was generated by Microsoft Word, but don't have it, then: [...] Suppose we have a file

example.pdf

First process it with the free version of InftyReader.
This will not give the TeX directly (except for the first page) but it will produce a file

example.pdf2txt

Then you can run AbiWord on this file:

abiword --to=tex example.pdf2txt

The result is not great, but not too bad either.

Bjørn Kjos-Hanssen's user avatar

A two step process has worked well for me.

Step 1) Convert PDF to DOCX (MS Word)
You can use pdf2docx or use the acrobat online service (if your doc is public)

Step 2) Convert the DOCX to LaTeX with pandoc.

pandoc -f docx -t latex --standalone -o converted.tex input.docx

Pandoc does not accept pdf as input and docx is a very rich format. So, this combination produces reasonably good results.

Mohsen Banan's user avatar

results.title: Typst: An easy to learn alternative for LaTex | Hacker News
results.content: target different output formats. | | | |  |  | aragilar on July 21, 2024  | root | parent | next (javascript:void(0))   I've done .doc -> .md -> .tex -> .pdf via pandoc (e.g. to convert a confluence document to a nicely typeset PDF), so I'm not sure there's much difference. Naturally, a format with more metadata loses metadata when converted to one with less (and the reverse, you may need to add metadata in when going up the chain), but unless your document is bound to a specific format (in which case, I'd pick RST for HTML and LaTeX for pdf), markdown gives you the content and pandoc lets you specify a template (and with pandoc, you can take the markdown and generate slides in HTML, PDF and create notes through the different outputs). | | | |  |  | constantcrying on July 21, 2024  | root [...] | |  |  | Typst: An easy to learn alternative for LaTex (github.com/typst) | | 649 points by vogu66 on July 20, 2024 | hide | past | favorite | 187 comments |     |  |  |  --- | | |  |  | mr-karan on July 20, 2024  | next (javascript:void(0))   Typst has been pretty amazing, and at my organization, we’re very happy with it. We needed to generate over 1.5 million PDFs every night and experimented with various solutions—from Puppeteer for HTML to PDF conversions, to pdflatex and lualatex. Typst has been several orders of magnitude faster and has a lighter resource footprint. Also, templating the PDFs in LaTeX wasn’t a pleasant developer experience, but with Typst templates, it has been quite intuitive. We’ve written more about this large-scale PDF generation stack in our blog here:  | | | | [...] output is phenomenal. Both  and  are rendered with lwarp and I daresay they are a noticeable improvement over the PDF equivalents. | | | |  |  | setopt on July 22, 2024  | root | parent | prev | next (javascript:void(0))   Might be worth mentioning that arXiv is doing mass conversion of TeX to HTML these days:  | | | |  |  | cactusfrog on July 20, 2024  | parent | prev | next (javascript:void(0))   Yes, this is one of the major problems with this. I don’t like pdfs | | | |  |  | Kinrany on July 20, 2024  | parent | prev | next (javascript:void(0))   Someone mentioned that pandoc works decently for this. | | | |  |  | ykonstant on July 20, 2024  | root | parent | next (javascript:void(0))   I am using pandoc to transfer some of my notes to the web:  | | | |  |  | nicf on July 20, 2024  |

results.title: How can I convert from Microsoft Word to a LaTeX document - TeX
results.content:
There will still be manual editing to do, but at least the major parts will be done for you - doc envelope, sectioning and other trivial stuff. So that you won't have to hunt a plain text file for the chapter/section titles.

LibreOffice will export directly to PDF too and read PDF into ODT which is useful when one wants to reverse-engineer a PDF into LaTeX.

ajeh's user avatar

If you're running an AppleScript-compatible operating system, I've written a script to do this. It has many limitations as far as pictures go (totally unsupported), but it handles the essentials (bold, italics, underscores, percent signs, dollar signs, tables (in tabu)). Note that it keeps everything in unicode, therefore the `fontspec` package is recommended with `xelatex`. It is a work in progress. [...] That said, converting a manuscript from Word to TeX is the kind of task where it might be worth opening up a virtual machine or going to a Windows laptop to complete.

`pandoc -s sourcefile.docx -o outputfile.tex`

The free open source word processor AbiWord has an MS Word import function, and, if you install it (be sure to check it under install time, or if on Linux, install the necessary plugin package), a LaTeX export function. It works decently well for simple documents.
